description: v4.1.2 Module 2 (Flop C-bet IP) seed scenarios — 24 hand-authored entries across 6 board buckets. NOT integrated into production data. See postflop-v4.1.2-module2-architecture.md and -schema-taxonomy.md for context. All scenarios use auditStatus='review_pending' and sourceConfidence='expert_judgment' (no solver runs were used). v0.1.1 fix-pass corrected mechanical errors in scenarios #11, #13, #21, #22, #24 (flush-vs-flush-draw mis-counts on monotone/two-tone boards) and labelling improvements in #2, #5, #6, #17, #18.

seedConventions.spot: NLH MTT, chipEV, 100BB, BTN open 2.5x vs BB call, single-raised pot, BTN in position, flop decision
seedConventions.sourceConfidence: expert_judgment (default for all v4.1.2 seeds; upgrade to consensus_gto requires GPT-review sign-off; upgrade to solver_verified requires a solver run)
seedConventions.auditStatus: review_pending (default for all v4.1.2 seeds; upgrade to approved requires audit-plan rules + GPT-review sign-off + human ratification)

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Ah, removing AK / Ax suited combos from BB

question.prompt: With AhKh on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair top kicker on a dry A-high board. Bet small with high frequency for value.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN has clear range and nut advantage on A-high dry rainbow boards. Small high-frequency c-bets dominate.
explanation.handLogic: AK is the top of BTN's range here. It wants to bet for value against worse Ax, K-high floats, and pocket pairs that defend. Slow-playing risks giving up streets where BB folds out worse hands cheaply.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts value from a wide thin-calling range without folding out the underpair / K-high portion. Big sizing chases away the marginal hands AK actually beats.
explanation.commonMistake: Overbetting with AK on dry A-high boards. The pot doesn't need to grow this fast and worse hands fold to big bets. Bet small with high frequency.
explanation.takeaway: Strong top pair on dry A-high → bet small with frequency.

question.prompt: With 7c6c on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with backdoors on dry A-high. Range-stab small as part of high-frequency c-bet.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN range-bets dry A-high boards at high frequency. Air hands with backdoor equity carry the bluff frequency.
explanation.handLogic: 76 has zero showdown value but enough backdoor equity (BDFD + BDSD with the 8 on board) to continue as a bluff if called. Checking gives up the stab equity for nothing.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps the cost of the bluff low while still folding out a meaningful portion of BB's air and weak underpairs. Big sizing burns chips on a hand with no realisation behind it.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting air on a dry A-high board. The board class wants small high-frequency, not polar-big.
explanation.takeaway: Dry A-high with air + backdoors → bet small as part of the range-stab.

question.prompt: With 9d9s on As 8d 3h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Medium pocket pair on A-high. Check for pot control — Ax calls, weak hands fold.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN's medium pairs (TT down to 22 below the ace) realise more equity by checking back on A-high boards than by betting.
explanation.handLogic: 99 beats only worse pocket pairs and missed broadways. Betting makes weaker hands fold (we get nothing) and stronger hands (Ax) call (we lose). Checking gets us to showdown cheaply.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting because we have a pair. Medium pairs on A-high should check back the majority of the time — pot control is the whole point.
explanation.takeaway: Mid pocket pair on A-high → check for pot control.

question.prompt: With QcJh on As 8d 3h, BTN's recommended action is to bet small as part of the range-stab. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Range advantage — bet most of the range cheaply
Value — get worse hands to call
Give up — no equity, save chips

explanation.short: Range stab — BTN's range advantage makes small high-frequency betting +EV even with no-equity hands.
explanation.rangeContext: Dry A-high boards favour BTN's whole range. The frame is range betting, not hand-by-hand bluffing.
explanation.handLogic: QJ has no made hand and no real draw, but BTN bets it as part of the range strategy: small sizing, high frequency, with the threat of further pressure on later streets.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling this a 'bluff' is technically correct but misses the strategic frame. The right mental model is range advantage, not isolated bluffing.
explanation.takeaway: On range-advantaged boards, the reason to bet air is range, not bluff.

question.prompt: With KsQc on Kh 9c 4s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair good kicker on K-high. Bet small for value and protection.
explanation.rangeContext: K-high rainbow boards are range-advantaged for BTN; small high-frequency betting is the dominant strategy.
explanation.handLogic: KQ is one of the strongest top pairs we routinely have here (AK 3-bet preflop a lot, so KQ becomes a top of our value range). Bet small to get value from worse Kx, second pairs, and protect against the few overcards (A only) that can outdraw us.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts the most calls from the long tail of bluff-catchers BB defends. Big sizing folds out worse hands and inflates the pot needlessly.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating KQ as worth a polar overbet. On rainbow K-high, range bet small.
explanation.takeaway: Top pair good kicker on dry K-high → bet small for value.

scenarios.blockerNote: Inside straight draw — Q completes K-Q-J-T-9; plus backdoor diamond flush draw and 2 cards (J and T) that pair below K

question.prompt: With JdTd on Kh 9c 4s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Overcard + gutshot + backdoor on K-high. Semi-bluff with the small range bet.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN's strategy is range bet small on dry K-high; JT carries a useful equity bundle (J overcard + Q gutshot + backdoor diamonds).
explanation.handLogic: JT has fold equity from the J overcard, plus 4 outs to a straight (Q), plus a backdoor flush draw. That's enough to bet as a semi-bluff and continue against most lines.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small bet keeps the cost of the bluff low and folds out air / weak underpairs. Big bet over-charges for a hand that doesn't have made-hand value.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting JT for fold equity. The board doesn't support a polar strategy yet — small range bet is correct.
explanation.takeaway: Overcard + gutshot + BDFD → small range bet as semi-bluff.

question.prompt: With 7h7c on Kh 9c 4s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Small underpair below 9. Check for pot control and showdown.
explanation.rangeContext: Below the second board card, 77 is a weak-showdown hand that wants to see the river cheaply.
explanation.handLogic: 77 beats only smaller pairs and pure air. Betting folds out hands we beat and gets called by the K-x and 9-x range that beats us. Check, plan to fold to one bet on most run-outs.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting every pocket pair to 'protect'. Below the second board card, the pair is too weak to bet for protection — you're protecting against hands that already beat you.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair below the second board card → check, accept showdown value.

scenarios.blockerNote: Ace blocker reduces BB's two-pair / nut combos slightly

question.prompt: With AcQh on Kh 9c 4s, BTN's recommended action is to bet small. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Range advantage — bet most of the range cheaply
Value — get worse hands to call
Blocker pressure — A-blocker cuts BB's calling range
Give up — no equity, save chips

explanation.short: Range stab on K-high; the A blocker is a small additional reason but not the primary one.
explanation.rangeContext: The strategic frame on K-high dry boards is the same as A-high dry: BTN range bets small at high frequency.
explanation.handLogic: AQ has 6 outs to a pair plus a small backdoor bundle. The decision to bet is driven by range advantage, not by the A blocker — the A blocker is a secondary reason that confirms the bet but isn't the primary engine.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling AQ a 'value bet' here. AQ doesn't beat much yet; it's an equity-realisation + range-stab hand.
explanation.takeaway: On range-advantaged boards, the primary reason to bet AQ is range, not value.

question.prompt: With JhJc on 8s 7d 5h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Overpair on a low-connected wet board where BB has range and nut advantage. Check.
explanation.rangeContext: 8-7-5 is the textbook check-heavy board for BTN. BB's flatting range is loaded with two-pair, sets, and straight combos; BTN's overpairs are bluff-catchers more than value hands.
explanation.handLogic: JJ on 8-7-5 is ahead of one pair but behind every two-pair, set, and made straight in BB's range. Betting bloats the pot for a hand that gets check-raised by everything that beats us. Check, see a turn, re-evaluate.
explanation.commonMistake: Betting big with overpairs 'for protection' on low-connected boards. This is one of the textbook critical leaks Module 2 is designed to flag — protection bets on these boards lose money against the hands that beat you.
explanation.takeaway: Overpair on 8-7-5-style boards → check, do not 'protect'.

question.prompt: With AhQc on 8s 7d 5h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Two overcards no equity on a board BB owns. Check, realise equity, plan to fold most turns.
explanation.rangeContext: Low connected boards favour BB; BTN should check the air portion of the range (no two-pair-plus, no real draw).
explanation.handLogic: AQ has 6 outs to top pair but the texture means even an A or Q doesn't make the best hand often. Bluffing here gets called or raised by the dense BB range — checking lets us realise equity without paying.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with AQ because 'BTN should c-bet'. Wrong board class — over-c-betting low connected boards is a textbook leak.
explanation.takeaway: Air on 8-7-5 → check, see if a free turn helps.

scenarios.blockerNote: 9-high made straight (5-6-7-8-9); second-nut straight behind T9 only

question.prompt: With 9c6c on 8s 7d 5h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Made straight (9-8-7-6-5). Bet big to charge worse made hands and balance the polar range.
explanation.rangeContext: On 8-7-5, BTN's value bets are concentrated in straights, sets, and two pair. Polar big sizing extracts maximum from BB's overpair / two pair calls.
explanation.handLogic: 96 made the second-nut straight. We have BB's two-pair / set range crushed and they will pay off a big bet. Checking back leaves money on the table.
explanation.sizingLogic: Big (~75-100%) maximises value from sets and overpairs that don't fold and balances our future polar-bluff frequency on this board.
explanation.commonMistake: Slow-playing the made straight. On wet textures, betting big extracts value from worse hands that will improve to second-best on the turn.
explanation.takeaway: Made straight on wet low connected → bet big polar.

question.prompt: With KsKd on 8s 7d 5h, BTN's recommended action is to check. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Pot control — overpair is a bluff-catcher here
Protection — bet big to deny equity
Value — get worse hands to call
Give up — fold to all pressure

explanation.short: KK is a bluff-catcher on 8-7-5. Check to keep the pot small, not to 'protect'.
explanation.rangeContext: BB's range hits this board hard. KK is ahead of single pairs and bluffs but behind two-pair, sets, and straights.
explanation.handLogic: Checking achieves pot control — we still beat BB's bluffs and underpairs but avoid bloating against the hands that beat us. The 'protection' frame is wrong here: we're not protecting equity, we're avoiding a check-raise.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling this a 'protection' check or, worse, making it a 'protection bet'. The classic critical leak is betting overpairs big on low connected boards in the name of protection.
explanation.takeaway: Overpair on low-connected wet → check is pot control, not protection.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Ah on a two-tone hearts board — blocks BB's nut flush draws. Hero has 1 heart + 2 board hearts = 3 hearts → BACKDOOR nut flush draw (NOT a real flush draw); needs runner-runner hearts.

question.prompt: With AhKc on Th 6h 2c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Two overcards + ace-blocker on two-tone T-high. Bet small as part of the range-stab; the Ah blocker is supporting evidence, not a polar-bluff trigger.
explanation.rangeContext: Two-tone T-high boards favour BTN slightly; modern strategy is small high-frequency betting with the air range that holds backdoor equity or relevant blockers. AhKc fits that air-with-blockers slot — it is NOT a real flush draw because hero has only 1 heart + 2 board hearts = 3 hearts (a backdoor draw, not a 4-card flush draw).
explanation.handLogic: AhKc has 6 outs to top pair (3 each for A and K) plus a backdoor flush draw and ace-blocker effect. That's enough equity + leverage to bet, but it is NOT a polar-bluff candidate — overestimating the equity by treating it as a real NFD is the mistake to avoid.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps the bet consistent with the range-stab strategy on T-high two-tone. Big sizing over-bluffs a hand that doesn't have made-hand or real-draw equity.
explanation.commonMistake: Confusing 'backdoor flush draw + ace blocker' with 'real nut flush draw'. With only 3 hearts in the hand+board, the flush is two cards away, not one. Polar-overbetting this hand leaks chips against BB's two-pair / set / flush-draw continuing range.
explanation.takeaway: Air with ace-blocker + backdoors on two-tone → small range stab, not polar bluff.

question.prompt: With Tc8s on Th 6h 2c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Top pair with a weak kicker on a flush-draw-rich board. Bet small for thin value + protection.
explanation.rangeContext: On two-tone T-high boards, BTN should still bet for value with top pairs but at the smaller sizing because BB's range contains many flush draws that won't fold to small.
explanation.handLogic: T8 is top pair with kicker concerns. We want value from BB's underpairs and 9-x and protection against the heart draws. Small accomplishes both without escalating the pot for a marginal hand.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) keeps draws calling at correct price for our equity defence and lets us fold without much loss to a check-raise. Big sizing inflates the pot for a hand that doesn't beat much when called heavily.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar overbetting weak top pair. Small high-frequency is the right size on two-tone with thin value hands.
explanation.takeaway: Weak top pair on two-tone → small for thin value + protection.

question.prompt: With 4d4c on Th 6h 2c (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Small underpair on a flush-draw board. Check, take cheap showdown when you can.
explanation.rangeContext: 44 is below most of BB's calling range and has no draw equity. The two-tone texture also means flushing turns / rivers will price us out anyway.
explanation.handLogic: Betting folds out hands we beat (worse underpairs, missed broadways) and gets called by everything that beats us (Tx, 6x, flush draws). Check, plan to fold most turns.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with small pocket pairs on two-tone boards 'because we have something'. The pair is too weak to bet, and the flush draw board makes our equity even thinner.
explanation.takeaway: Small underpair on two-tone → check, accept the showdown.

question.prompt: With 9h8h on Th 6h 2c, BTN's recommended action is to bet (commonly bet big). What is the main reason?

choices.label: Equity realization — strong draw wants to bet for fold equity + value when it hits
Value — get worse hands to call
Thin value — extract from worse marginal hands
Blocker pressure — block opponent's strong calls

explanation.short: Combo draw with raw equity. Bet big to combine fold equity with realisation when the draw hits.
explanation.rangeContext: Combo draws are the prime semi-bluff candidates on two-tone wet boards. Strategic frame is equity realisation, not value.
explanation.handLogic: 9h8h has flush draw + gutshot (7 makes 6-7-8-9-10) plus 2 pair outs. Betting now gives us fold equity AND we still have 12+ outs when called.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling this a value bet. We have no made hand — the bet is a semi-bluff with equity realisation as the strategic engine.
explanation.takeaway: Combo draws bet for equity realisation, not value.

question.prompt: With QhQc on Kc Kd 7s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: QQ on paired K-high. Bet small for thin value — but checking is also fully GTO, since QQ is in the in-between region.
explanation.rangeContext: Paired K-high boards favour BTN heavily. Small high-frequency c-bets work, and QQ is one of the hands that mixes between bet_small and check in modern solver outputs.
explanation.handLogic: QQ is ahead of TT, JJ, 99, 88, and most 7-x; behind only KK, AA, AK, K-x trips. Betting small extracts value from the long bluff-catcher tail; checking realises showdown equity cheaply when BB does not pay off (BB's continuing range vs a small bet is mostly worse pairs and ace-high floats).
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) gets called by 88-JJ and ace-high floats. Check is GTO-defensible because QQ is too thin for big and the value tail vs small isn't huge.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar overbetting QQ. The right frame on paired K-high is small high-frequency, not polar big — and check is acceptable when balancing the checking range.
explanation.takeaway: QQ on paired K-high → small for thin value, with check fully acceptable.

scenarios.blockerNote: Ace blocker — BB rarely has AK in the calling range; A overcard adds equity outs

question.prompt: With AsQs on Kc Kd 7s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Air with A blocker on paired K-high. Range stab small — range advantage is the engine, blocker is supporting evidence.
explanation.rangeContext: BTN range bets paired K-high small at high frequency; AQ carries A blocker plus 6 pair outs as the bluff component, but the strategic frame is range advantage, not isolated blocker betting.
explanation.handLogic: AQ has no made hand but the A blocker reduces some of BB's strongest continuing combos and the A overcard gives us a turn equity bump. The primary reason to bet, however, is range advantage: BTN bets paired K-high small with a wide range and AQ slots into the bluff portion of that range strategy.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) is consistent with the value range we'd bet here (overpairs, trips). Big sizing isolates us against trips that never fold.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting AQ as a bluff. Wrong size — paired K-high is a small high-frequency board class.
explanation.takeaway: A-blocker air on paired K-high → small range stab.

question.prompt: With 6c6d on Kc Kd 7s (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Small underpair below 7. Check, accept showdown — betting folds out worse and gets called by 7-x and trips.
explanation.rangeContext: On paired K-high, BTN bets small with a wide value+bluff range. Sub-7 underpairs are the showdown floor that prefers checking.
explanation.handLogic: 66 beats only 22-55 and pure air. Better to check, see a turn, fold to most pressure than to bet and either fold out worse or get called by 7-x trips and overpairs.
explanation.commonMistake: Auto-c-betting any pocket pair on paired boards. Below the bottom unpaired card, the pair is a check-take-showdown hand.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair below the bottom card on paired board → check.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Kh — blocks a key trips combo from BB

question.prompt: With AhKh on Kc Kd 7s, BTN's recommended strategy mixes between bet_small and check. When BTN does check, what is the main reason?

choices.label: Pot control — keep pot small with marginal value
Value — get worse to call later

explanation.short: Trip Ks + ace kicker. Checking is for delayed value — let BB barrel turn or give us a free card to extract more later.
explanation.rangeContext: On paired Kx, the strongest hands like AhKh mix between betting for thin range value and checking to balance the checking range and induce.
explanation.handLogic: We crush BB's calling range. Betting small extracts thin value; checking lets BB take a stab on the turn or makes it harder for them to fold middling pairs and ace-high floats. Both lines are correct — the check line aims at delayed value, not pot control.
explanation.commonMistake: Calling the check 'give up' or 'pot control'. With trips top kicker, we're trapping for value, not surrendering or controlling pot size.
explanation.takeaway: Strong made hand on paired board → checking is for value (trap), not for pot control.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Ah on a monotone hearts board. Ah is the nut card — when the flush completes, hero has the nut flush. Hero has 1 heart + 3 board hearts = 4 hearts → real flush draw (need 1 more heart on turn or river). NOT a made flush.

question.prompt: With AhTd on Jh 8h 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Nut flush draw on monotone — bet small as part of the small-frequency strategy on monotone, or check to balance the checking range.
explanation.rangeContext: Monotone boards reward small sizings. The Ah is the nut card; hero has 4 hearts in hand+board (1 hero + 3 board) → real flush draw needing one more heart. Hero blocks BB's nut combos and has nut equity if the draw completes.
explanation.handLogic: AhTd has 9 outs to the nut flush, plus the Ah blocker effect on BB's continuing range. It's a strong semi-bluff candidate, but on monotone the dominant sizing is small (rather than polar big), because big chases away the one-pair / lower-FD calling range.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) extracts thin equity-denial value from one-pair-with-heart calls and lower flush draws while preserving fold equity. Big sizing is dominated on monotone — it only gets called by made flushes (we lose) and folds out the marginal calls.
explanation.commonMistake: Polar-overbetting the nut flush draw on monotone, or worse, treating the Ah as if it already gave us a made nut flush (it doesn't — we have 4 of one suit, not 5).
explanation.takeaway: Nut flush DRAW on monotone (not made) → small bet keeps marginal calls in and preserves nut blocker pressure.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds Kh on monotone hearts — second-nut FLUSH DRAW (1 hero heart + 3 board hearts = 4 hearts → need 1 more heart). Kh blocker reduces some opponent K-flush combos; Q overcard adds 3 outs. NOT a made flush.

question.prompt: With KhQd on Jh 8h 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: K-flush DRAW (not made) on monotone. Mixed strategy — bet small at moderate frequency or check for equity realization.
explanation.rangeContext: On monotone J-high, BTN's strategy is check-heavy. K-FD is a second-nut FD with the Kh blocker — it has equity but is dominated by AhX nut FDs in BB's range. Modern solver outputs mix between small bet and check.
explanation.handLogic: KhQd has 9 outs to a K-high or higher flush, plus 3 outs to a Q-pair (some of which improve us to top pair). The Kh blocker reduces a small slice of BB's continuing range. But the hand is dominated when the flush completes — Ah-X always beats us — so betting big is wrong, and check is fully GTO.
explanation.sizingLogic: Small (~33%) at moderate frequency is fine for the bet-portion of the mix, taking thin equity-denial value. Big is wrong because it inflates the pot when we get called by hands that mostly beat us (made flushes).
explanation.commonMistake: Treating Kh on monotone hearts as a 'made K-flush.' Hero has 4 hearts (1 hero + 3 board) — that's a flush DRAW, not a made flush. Need a 5th heart on turn or river.
explanation.takeaway: K-flush DRAW on monotone → mixed: small bet or check; bet_big is rarely correct.

scenarios.blockerNote: Holds zero hearts — no flush blocker

question.prompt: With 9d9c on Jh 8h 4h (BTN open vs BB call, 100BB SRP), what should BTN do most often?

explanation.short: Underpair with zero hearts on monotone. Check, accept showdown — no flush, no blocker, no bet.
explanation.rangeContext: Without a heart, our equity to make the flush is 0. We're a pure bluff-catcher against any heart and a small favourite over air.
explanation.handLogic: 99 beats 22-77 with no heart and pure air. Betting only gets called by hands that beat us (any heart, J-x, sets). Check, fold to most pressure.
explanation.commonMistake: Stabbing with underpairs on monotone 'because we have a pair'. Without a flush blocker, the pair is too vulnerable.
explanation.takeaway: Underpair with no heart on monotone → check, don't bluff yourself.

scenarios.blockerNote: Hero has 1 heart (6h) + 3 board hearts = 4 hearts → flush DRAW (not made). When the flush completes, the 5-card flush is at-best Jh-high (the highest heart in the 5-card combo), and any opponent heart higher than 6 dominates this draw. Plus a gutshot to 7 for 6-7-8-9 straight (or straight flush if 7h hits).

question.prompt: With 6h5c on Jh 8h 4h, BTN's recommended action is to check despite holding a flush draw plus a gutshot. What is the main reason?

choices.label: Give up — flush draw is reverse-dominated by every higher heart in BB's range
Pot control — keep pot small with marginal equity
Value — bet to get worse to call
Equity realization — bet to leverage fold equity

explanation.short: Low flush DRAW is reverse-dominated by every higher heart in BB's range — check to give up cheaply.
explanation.rangeContext: On monotone hearts, hero with only the 6h holds the lowest possible flush DRAW (not a made flush — hero has 4 hearts, needs 5). When the flush completes on turn or river, every higher heart in BB's range (7h-Ah) beats us.
explanation.handLogic: 6h5c has 9 outs to complete the flush, but most of those outs make a hand that LOSES to BB's higher heart draws or holdings. Plus the gutshot (7) adds 4 outs (3 if we don't double-count 7h). Net effective equity is much lower than a normal FD because of the reverse-domination problem. Betting bluffs into a stronger range; checking gives up cheaply.
explanation.commonMistake: Treating the 6h on monotone as a 'made low flush' (it isn't — it's a low FD), or betting the FD 'for fold equity' when most of the outs we hit are dominated. Worst of all: betting for 'value' — we have neither a made hand nor positive equity vs calling ranges.
explanation.takeaway: Reverse-dominated low flush draw on monotone → check / give up; betting for value is the trap to avoid.
